brief_document.title: Error taxonomy: ActivityError and engine-originated failures
brief_document.purpose: Express the retryable-vs-terminal classification in Gleam's type system, mapping aion-core's ActivityError, and define the distinct typed failures the engine originates (timeout, cancellation, non-determinism, decode) so an author never confuses an author-decided failure with an engine-decided one.
brief_document.task: Define aion/error: ActivityError with Retryable and Terminal constructors carrying a message and structured details, mapping aion-core's classification. Define the engine-originated failure types (timeout, cancellation, non-determinism, decode) as distinct typed variants. Establish the no-panic / typed-Result discipline for the whole package.

requirements.title: ActivityError retryable/terminal split
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define ActivityError with Retryable and Terminal constructors carrying a message and structured details, expressing aion-core's retryable/terminal classification.
acceptance: error.gleam defines ActivityError with constructors Retryable(message, details) and Terminal(message, details).
A doc comment states the engine retries on Retryable until the RetryPolicy's attempts are exhausted and fails immediately on Terminal, mapping aion-core's classification (aion-core C21).
Helper constructors (e.g. retryable(message), terminal(message)) make the common case ergonomic.
A unit test pattern-matches both constructors and asserts the classification is preserved.

requirements.title: Engine-originated failure types
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define the engine-originated failures (timeout, cancellation, non-determinism, decode) as distinct typed variants, separate from author-returned ActivityError.
acceptance: Distinct typed variants exist for timeout, cancellation, non-determinism violation, and decode failure (the latter wrapping the DecodeError from AF-002), used by the workflow primitives' return types (e.g. ReceiveError, the with_timeout result).
These are not ActivityError constructors — an author cannot construct an engine-originated failure as if it were an activity decision, and a workflow handling code can distinguish them by type.
A doc comment relates non-determinism to AD's NonDeterminismError and timeout/cancellation to the engine lifecycle, so the provenance is clear.

requirements.title: No-panic, typed-Result discipline
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL establish that no public function panics, calls todo, or uses assert as control flow; every fallible operation returns a typed Result.
acceptance: error.gleam documents the package-wide discipline: failure is always a typed Result error, never a panic or todo.

The error types defined here are sufficient to express every documented failure path of the later primitives (activity failure, decode failure, timeout, cancellation, non-determinism, unknown query/signal).
Review confirms no panic / todo / assert-as-control-flow is introduced by this brief.

boundaries: SHALL NOT define RetryPolicy or Backoff — those are AF-004 (they reference the classification defined here).
SHALL NOT define the activity or workflow primitives — only the error types they return.
SHALL NOT re-implement aion-core's Rust ActivityError — this is the Gleam mirror that maps to it across the ffi boundary.
verification: gleam test passes for the classification tests.
No panic, todo, or assert-as-control-flow in new code.

checklist.text: ActivityError has Retryable and Terminal constructors carrying a message and structured details, mapping aion-core's retryable/terminal classification.
Engine-originated failures (timeout, cancellation, non-determinism, decode) are distinct typed variants, separate from author-returned ActivityError.
No public function panics, calls todo, or uses assert as control flow; every fallible operation returns a typed Result.

stories.text: As a workflow author, I want to classify an activity failure as retryable or terminal once, in the type system, so that the engine applies my retry policy correctly without me re-stating the intent.

constraints.text: Pure Gleam package. No Rust, no engine code, no beamr dependency inside aion_flow. It publishes to Hex standalone (gleam_stdlib is its only runtime dependency, plus gleam_json for the default JSON codecs).
Every @external(erlang, ...) declaration lives in the single internal module aion/internal/ffi. No author-facing module declares an @external (per D1).

Module files contain logic; the package root and any mod-equivalent re-export aggregator stay declarations/re-exports only. No god files — split before a module exceeds ~500 lines of code (excluding comments/whitespace).

No partial implementations. Every public function is complete and total: no panic, no todo, no assert-as-control-flow; failure paths return typed Result errors. Gleam has no exceptions for control flow — every fallible binding returns Result.

Type safety is enforced, not advisory: activity input/output, signal payload, query return, and child input/output types are static type parameters; an invalid composition fails `gleam build`. A type-safety test brief asserts representative invalid compositions are rejected (compile-fail expectations documented).

Determinism: the SDK exposes workflow.now/workflow.random bound to AD's determinism context and exposes NO binding to a wall clock or entropy source. Workflow-code documentation states the prohibition explicitly.

The recorded/side-effectful boundary is structural: the only side-effect dispatch the SDK offers is workflow.run(activity) (and all/race/map over activities). There is no generic side_effect(fn) escape hatch (per D4).

No hardcoded defaults for configurable values: no default RetryPolicy, no default timeout, no default backoff numbers baked into the SDK. An activity without a retry decorator runs once; the author supplies all policy values (CLAUDE.md no-assumed-defaults).
User data crosses the boundary only through a Codec(a); a decode failure is a typed DecodeError, never a panic. No primitive silently swallows a decode error.

aion_flow does not implement engine mechanics (timer firing, signal routing, replay) — those are AE/AD/AT, reached via @external. It does not author NIFs in Rust — that is AN. It is not packaged into .aion — that is AP. The cluster owns only the Gleam API surface and its binding declarations.
All acceptance uses Gleam tooling: gleam build, gleam check, gleam format --check, gleam test must all pass clean. The test harness (aion/testing) runs under gleam test with no external services.

resolved_context.intention: Define aion_flow, the Gleam package (published to Hex) that workflow authors import to write durable workflows. It is the human-facing surface of the entire engine: a workflow author writes a plain, statically-typed Gleam function — decisions, loops, data transformation — and reaches the outside world only through this SDK's activity, signal, query, timer, and child-workflow primitives. The package is pure Gleam with no engine code inside it; every primitive is a thin @external(erlang) binding that resolves at runtime, when beamr loads the compiled workflow and the engine has registered the matching NIFs (AE-004), to the engine-side mechanics in AE/AD/AT. The defining property is compile-time type safety: activity inputs and outputs, signal payloads, and query returns are all statically typed, so an invalid composition — passing a String where an activity expects an Order, awaiting a signal of the wrong type, returning the wrong type from a query handler — fails at `gleam build`, not three days into a long-running production workflow. This is the concrete advantage over Temporal's SDKs (Go weakly typed, TypeScript better but runtime-checked). The package also ships aion/testing, the Temporal-equivalent test harness: simulate time so a month-long sleep resolves instantly, mock activities to return canned results, and assert that a workflow replays to the same state — all in plain gleam test, with no engine, no beamr, and no store.
